setting.note: In 1944 the fourteen-year-old Budapest Jew György Köves is pulled off a bus, deported, and survives Auschwitz, Buchenwald and the Zeitz labour camp with a bewildered, matter-of-fact detachment, before returning to a city that wants him to forget. Kertész, who lived it, maps the real geography of the catastrophe: the yellow-star house and the assembly points of Budapest, and the camps to the north where a boy learns to take each step of the machinery as, somehow, natural.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Imre Kertész, first published 1975 (Sorstalanság). Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.

palette.note: The flat grey of the boy's terrible neutrality, taking each stage of the machinery as ordinary, the tone that gives the book its shattering force. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
